coredata.dc:title: A unique dual-excitation carbon quantum dots: Facile synthesis and application as a dual-“on-off-on” fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ouwen
Wan, Shuyu
Zhang, Yuheng
Li, Jiawei
Zhu, Xiashi
coredata.dc:description: A dual-"on-off-on" fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BG-NCDs) with the dual-excitation property was developed, which had a blue emission peak at 398 nm and a green emission peak at 518nm when excited at 298nm and 404nm, respectively. BG-NCDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, and FT-IR. The results showed that (1) the Fe (III) and ascorbic acid (AA) could “turn off/turn on” the blue fluorescence of BG-NCDs, the blue fluorescence of BG-NCDs was used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe to detecting the Fe (III) and AA in the linear ranges of 0.04–20.00 mg/L and 0.57–10.00 mg/L, the LODs were as low as 0.012 mg/L and 0.19 mg/L; (2) the presence of Cu (II) and glutathione (GSH) could “turn off/turn on” the green fluorescence of BG-NCDs, the “on-off-on” process of green fluorescence of BG-NCDs could be established as a fluorescence probe to detecting Cu (II) and GSH in the linear ranges of 1.08–16.00 mg/L and 2.46–32.00 mg/L, with LODs of 0.36 mg/L and 0.86 mg/L. The method was applied to determination analytes in real samples with satisfactory results. BG-NCDs displayed low cytotoxicity and was multicolored imaging DU145 cells successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Dual-excitation to dual-emission
'On-off-on' fluorescent probe
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Fe (III)/AA analysis
FLG “on-off-on” determination of Cu (II) and GSH
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Cu (II)/GSH analysis
Determination of real samples with fluorescence probe “off”
Determination of real samples with fluorescence probe “on”

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of BG-NCDs (inset: HRTEM of BG-NCDs (left) and particle size distribution of BG-NCDs (right)); (b) FT-IR spectra of BG-NCDs, EDA and diphenylsemicarbazide; (c) XPS spectrum BG-NCDs; (d) High-resolution XPS of C1 s spectra of BG-NCDs; (e) High-resolution XPS of N1 s spectra of BG-NCDs; (f) High-resolution XPS of O1 s spectra of BG-NCDs
(a) Fluorescence spectra of BG-NCDs at different excitation wavelengths (inset a: fluorescence of BG-NCDs under excitation of 298 nm and 404 nm);
(b) UV–vis absorption (red line), excitation and emission spectra of BG-NCDs (FLB: blue line, FLG: green line);

(c) “On-off-on” schematic diagram of FLB (Fe (III): 20.0: mg/L, AA: 10.0 mg/L) (inset c: The illustration showed the higher concentration of Fe (III) and AA were added to the system until it was completely quenching and restore);

(d) “On-off-on” schematic diagram of FLG (Cu (II):16.0: mg/L, GSH 24.0 mg/L) (inset d: The illustration showed the higher concentration of Cu (II) and GSH were added to the system until it was completely quenching and restore)

(a) FLB quenching Stern-Volmer plot of BG-NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe (III) at different temperatures; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-NCDs, Fe (III), BG-NCDs + Fe (III), BG-NCDs+Fe (III)+AA; (c) Zeta potentials of BG-NCDs, BG-NCDs+Fe (III), BG-NCDs+Fe (III) + AA; (d) FLG quenching Stern-Volmer plot of BG-NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu (II) at different temperatures; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-NCDs, Cu (II), BG-NCDs+Cu (II), BG-NCDs+ Cu (II)+ GSH; f) Zeta potentials of BG-NCDs, BG-NCDs+Cu (II), BG-NCDs+Cu (II)+GSH

(a) Viability values (%) of cells estimated by MTT assay versus incubation concentrations of the BG-NCDs; (b) Bright-field of DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs; (c) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs (blue channel); (d) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs+Fe (III) (blue channel); (e) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs+Fe (III)+AA (blue channel); (f) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs (green channel); (g) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs+Cu (II) (green channel); (h) DU145 cells incubated with BG-NCDs+Cu (II)+GSH (green channel)
Detection of Fe (III) and Cu (II) in environmental water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ND meant it was not detected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of AA and GSH in food samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The results were converted according to the original food quality.

head.ce:title: A unique dual-excitation carbon quantum dots: Facile synthesis and application as a dual-“on-off-on” fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, College of Guangling, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, 225002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, College of Guangling, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, 225002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-"on-off-on" fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BG-NCDs) with the dual-excitation property was developed, which had a blue emission peak at 398 nm and a green emission peak at 518nm when excited at 298nm and 404nm, respectively. BG-NCDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, and FT-IR. The results showed that (1) the Fe (III) and ascorbic acid (AA) could “turn off/turn on” the blue fluorescence of BG-NCDs, the blue fluorescence of BG-NCDs was used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe to detecting the Fe (III) and AA in the linear ranges of 0.04–20.00 mg/L and 0.57–10.00 mg/L, the LODs were as low as 0.012 mg/L and 0.19 mg/L; (2) the presence of Cu (II) and glutathione (GSH) could “turn off/turn on” the green fluorescence of BG-NCDs, the “on-off-on” process of green fluorescence of BG-NCDs could be established as a fluorescence probe to detecting Cu (II) and GSH in the linear ranges of 1.08–16.00 mg/L and 2.46–32.00 mg/L, with LODs of 0.36 mg/L and 0.86 mg/L. The method was applied to determination analytes in real samples with satisfactory results. BG-NCDs displayed low cytotoxicity and was multicolored imaging DU145 cells successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as fluorescent nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm, had attracted extensive attention since it was discovered in 2004 [1]. Because of their excellent properties, such as adjustable fluorescence emission, good water solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, good chemical inertness, and environmental friendliness [2], CDs were considered to be a promising nanomaterial in chemical sensing [3], biosensor [4], cell imaging [5], and medical diagnosis [6], etc. In general, CDs were synthesized with a series of “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [7] The main synthesis methods include the hydrothermal method [8] and the microwave-assisted method [9].

The optical properties of CDs could be improved by functionalization [10]. Element doping (mainly including N [11], S [12], P [13], etc.) was a method to adjust the optical properties of CDs. Heteroatoms could not only change the electronic structure of CDs to enhance the adsorption capacity of CDs [14] but also could increase the fluorescence quantum yield by changing the surface state of CDs [15]. With the development of CDs research, more and more studies have begun researching the multicolor fluorescence property of CDs [16]. At present, the study of preparing CDs with different single-excitation to single-emission wavelengths under the same raw materials by controlling the reaction conditions had been developed [17], and there were also many studies seek to combine with different materials to functionalize CDs with fluorescence property of single-excitation to dual-emission such as CDs-Au NC composite and CDs-Eu-MOFs [18,19]. But, the development of dual-excitation to dual-emission property of CDs seems to be lacking [20].

Single-emission CDs were usually used as "on-off" fluorescent probes for direct quenching detection [21]. On this basis, the CDs could continue to develop to a novel "on-off-on" fluorescent probe [22], which another analyte could recover the fluorescence of CDs due to its stronger affinity or reactivity toward the first analyte [23]. There was the advantage of more analytes of the “on-off-on” fluorescent probe and had been successfully applied to analysis drugs [24] and biomolecule [25]. Functional CDs with single-excitation to dual-emission were usually used as ratiometric fluorescent probes in the analysis [26], which provided convenience in visual detection but still lack in the number of analytes. The novel dual-“on-off-on” probes with the advantage in the number of analytes could be developed based on fluorescence dual-excitation to dual-emission CDs. Dual-“on-off-on” fluorescent probes were equivalent to the existence of two independent fluorescent probes for the analysis different substances when dual-excitation to dual-emission CDs were used as fluorescent probes, which has the advantages in the number of detected substances and the convenience of independent detection of multiple substances. Besides, this probe had the advantage of adjustable fluorescence color in polychromatic imaging of cells [20]. The preparation of CDs with the unique property of fluorescence dual-excitation to dual-emission and used as dual-“on-off-on” fluorescent probes had not been reported.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BG-NCDs) with the property of dual-excitation to dual-emission was prepared by the hydrothermal method. There were blue and green emission peaks of BG-NCDs under two different excitation wavelengths(398 nm/298 nm,518 nm/404 nm). The presence of Fe (III)/Cu (II) can quench (“turn off”) the blue/green fluorescence respectively, the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH) can recover (“turn on”) the fluorescence of blue and green. The dual-“on-off-on” fluorescent probe was used to analysis Fe (III)/AA and Cu (II)/GSH in the real samples. Finally, the imaging of DU145 cells by this fluorescent probe was preliminarily discussed.

Diphenylsemicarbazide was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased by Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. Ascorbic acid and glutathione were purchased from Shanghai Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium acetate, glacial acetic acid, ammonium chloride, and ammonia were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fe (III) standard solution and Cu (II) standard solution were purchased from Beijing North Weiye Measurement Technology Research Institute (Beijing, China). Fruit and vitamin C effervescent tablets were purchased from a local supermarket in Yangzhou, China, and used immediately. The lake water was derived from Yangzhou Slender West Lake. DU145 was from Shanghai Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum was purchased from Thermo Fisher technologies, Inc. (Thermo, USA).
All reagents were analytical reagent grade, and the water used in the experiment was ultrapure.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on the Thermo-1780 UV–vis spectrophotometer (TMO, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer (Brooke, Germany). The hydrothermal reaction was carried out in DZF-6022 vacuum drying oven (Shanghai Yiheng, China). TEM was recorded on JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan Electronics, Japan). HRTEM was recorded on Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo, USA). Zeta potential was measured by ZEN3690 potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). The absorbance of metformin was recorded in the microplate reader EL800 (BioTek Instruments, USA). Cellular imaging was performed using Nikon eclipse Ti-s inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan).

In this experiment, BG-NCDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.30 g diphenylsemicarbazide and 1.0 mL EDA were mixed into a 30 mL solution. Then the solution was transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MW =1000 Da) for 24 h while ultrapure water was changed in an interval of 4 h. Then, the solution was purified through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. Finally, the BG-NCDs solution (100 mg/30 mL) was obtained and stored at 4 °C for future analysis.

1.0 mL BG-NCDs solution was mixed with the buffer solution and detection solution to form a 4 mL solution system. The fluorescence spectra of Fe (III) and AA in solution were recorded under the conditions that excitation wavelength was set at 298 nm, and emission wavelength was set at 398 nm, the slits were set to 5.0 and 10.0. The fluorescence spectra of Cu (II) and GSH in solution were recorded under the conditions that excitation wavelength was set at 404 nm and emission wavelength was set at 518 nm, the slits were set to 10.0 and 10.0.

Fluorescence quantum yield was generally measured by the reference method [12]. At the same excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity and the area of the fluorescence spectrum of diluted solutions of sample and reference substance were measured respectively. Then the absorbance of Uv–vis at the same excitation wavelength was measured. These values were substituted for the formula. The formula was as follow:
φ f = Y s ×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
Where φf represents the quantum yield, Ys is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 in water), F corresponds to the measured integrated emission intensity, A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength respectively. The subscript "s" refers to the reference substance and "u" refers to the sample.
The collected environmental water samples were used after filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane.

Fresh fruits (kiwi and tomato) were prepared as follows: 50.0 g fruit samples were randomly selected and mixed with 50 mL 0.001 mol/L HCl solution. The suspension was obtained by squeezing the mixture through a juicer and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. Then the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. Finally, the samples were collected and used for determination.

4.0 g vitamin C effervescent tablets were dissolved in 50 mL 0.001 mol/L HCl solution. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. Finally, the samples were collected and used for determination.

MTT assay was applied to determine cell viability. Briefly, DU145 cells (7 × 103) were incubated with BG-NCDs for 24 h at various concentrations. Control cells were given culture media containing 0.1 % DMSO. After incubation for 24 h, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT was added to cells, then cells were incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. Absorbance (A) of the final formazan was measured at 570 nm using Universal Microplate Reader EL800. Cell proliferation inhibition rate was calculated by the following formula:
C e l l
s u r v i v a l
r a t e =
O D
e x p e r i m e n t
O D
( c o n t r o l )
×
100 %
Where OD (experiment) represents the optical density of experiment group of cells and OD (control) represents the optical density of control group of cells

The DU145 cells were seeded on a 12-well plate and supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum, 100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 U/mL streptomycins. After cultured for 24 h in a humidified CO2 (5%) incubator at 37 °C, introduced the BG-NCDs and other substances into 12-well plates as required and incubated for a certain time. Finally, the cells were observed by the fluorescent inverted microscope. According to the instrument parameters, the excitation wavelength of the blue channel was 330−380 nm, the emission wavelength was 420 nm, and the excitation wavelength of the green channel was 450−490 nm, the emission wavelength was 520 nm.

The morphology, structure, surface functionalities, and chemical composition of the BG-NCDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, HRTEM), FT-IR spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

TEM images (Fig. 1 a) show that the BG-NCDs were spherical shape and evenly distributed on the copper grid. The size distribution of BG-NCDs ranged from 3 nm to 4 nm with the average diameter of 3.6 nm (right inset: Fig. 1a). HRTEM image (left inset: Fig. 1a) indicates that BG-NCDs had crystalline lattice fringes (0.21 nm) which could be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene.

FT-IR spectra of BG-NCDs (curve 1), EDA (curve 2), and diphenylsemicarbazide (curve 3) were shown in Fig. 1b. Compared with curve 3, BG-NCDs presented the obvious changes that the characteristic peaks of the benzene ring in the wavenumber of 1490 cm−1-1715 cm−1 almost disappeared, which indicated that the diphenylsemicarbazide had been carbonized and reconstituted to the carbon nucleus. The CN stretching vibration peak at 1273 cm−1 of EDA (curve 2) was retained in BG-NCDs (curve 1), indicating that the nitrogen was successfully doped into CDs. Besides, it could be concluded from curve1 that the peak located at 1435 cm−1 of BG-NCDs corresponded to CC bending vibration, and the peak located at 1273 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. The wide absorption band from 3160 cm−1 to 3678 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of NH and OH, and the absorption peak at 1682 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CO. These results indicated that abundant hydrophilic groups existed on the surface of BG-NCDs, including OH, COOH, and NH2.

The chemical composition of BG-NCDs was investigated by XPS. Fig. 1c was clearly shown the BG-NCDs were mainly composed of C (59.53 %), N (7.26 %), and O (33.21 %) elements with binding energy at 284.32, 400.04, and 531.67 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) can be decomposed into three peaks at 284.13, 286.08, and 288.33 eV, which were assigned to the CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively. Two peaks in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) could be attributed to C-N-C (399.48 eV) and C-N(401.08 eV). Furthermore, the high-resolved O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) exhibited the distinctive peaks of CO and CO at 531.63 and 533.13 eV, respectively. These results were in good agreement with the FT-IR analysis, which further confirmed the abundance of OH, COOH, and NH on the surface of prepared BG-NCDs.

The optical properties of BG-NCDs were investigated by fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence emission spectra of BG-NCDs under excitations of 290−470 nm were shown in Fig. 2 a. It could be seen that BG-NCDs presented two independent emission peaks with blue fluorescence (FLB) and green fluorescence (FLG), respectively (insert: Fig. 2a). Which indicated that BG-NCDs were the unique property of dual-excitation to dual-emission. Besides, FLB displayed excitation-dependent character and FLG displayed excitation-independent character. The above phenomenon could be attributed to the size distribution and similar surface states of the BG-NCDs [27–29].

There were two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 420 nm in UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-NCDs (Fig. 2b), which correspond to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively [30,31]. These results were consistent with the fluorescence spectra.
The optimal dual-excitation / dual-emission were 298/398 nm with FLB (φf = 24.2 %) and 404/518 nm with FLG (φf = 9.6 %) respectively.
The fluorescence spectra of 298/398 nm with FLB and 404/518 nm with FLG were independent, which can be used as "on-off-on" fluorescent probes to detect different analytes.

The FLB emission spectra were recorded at 398 nm under excitation of 298 nm. It could be seen from Fig. 2c that the FLB intensity was efficiently decreased in presence of Fe (III), which indicated that Fe (III) can effectively quench the FLB of BG-NCDs and "turn off" the FLB emission signal (curve 2). The intensity of FLB increased after AA adding to the solution, which meant that AA could restore the FLB and "turn on" the signal (curve 3). Therefore, the BG-NCDs can be used as an "on-off-on" fluorescent probe for Fe (III) and AA.

Fig. 2d showed the FLG emission spectra after Cu (II) and GSH were added to BG-NCDs solution which recorded at 512 nm under the excitation wavelength of 404 nm. The presence of Cu (II) could decrease the intensity of FLG, which indicated that Cu (II) could “turn off” the FLG emission signal (curve 2). The addition of GSH can completely restore the FLG, indicating that GSH could “turn on” the signal (curve 3). It can be concluded that the BG-NCDs could also be used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for Cu (II) and GSH.
In this experiment, the peaks of FLB and FLG were selected as “on-off-on” fluorescent probes to analysis Fe (III)/AA and Cu (II)/GSH, respectively.

The detection conditions of pH, time, temperature, and ionic strength were optimized by recording the difference of fluorescence intensity (ΔFB-off, ΔFB-on) before and after adding Fe (III) and AA under different conditions.

The effect of pH on ΔFB-off and ΔFB-on were shown in Fig. S1a. It can be seen that there was the same trend of ΔFB-off and ΔFB-on which increased in the pH range of 3.0–7.0 and then decreased in the pH range of 7.0–11.0. The reason for the phenomenon may be that (1) the amino group and the carboxyl group on the surface of BG-NCDs tended to increasingly deprotonate in the pH range of 3.0–7.0, leading to the enhancement of the interaction between BG-NCDs and Fe (III) (ΔFB-off↑) [25], (2) the ascorbate anion (AH−, with pKa1 of 4.25) was the predominant species of AA when pH > pKa1, and it has a higher ability of AA to reduce Fe (III) (ΔFB-on↑)due to it was easily deprotonated to radical cation in the medium of pH > 4.25 [32]; when the pH range was 7.0–11.0, (3) the side-reaction coefficient of hydroxyl increased, which weakened the interaction between BG-NCDs and Fe (III) (ΔFB-off↓) [33](4) the interaction between AA and Fe (III) weakened because the enediol group and lactone group of AA was easily oxidized in the alkaline medium (ΔFB-on↓) [34]. The results above could be concluded that pH 7.0 was the optimum pH for the experiment.

The effect of incubation time on ΔFB-off and ΔFB-on were shown in Fig. S1b. The ΔFB-off and the ΔFB-on both leveled to a stable value after a while. Hence, the FLB quenching time of 10 min and recovery time of 15 min was chosen for incubation.

From the experiments, there was little effect of ionic strength on the determination process and ΔFB-off decreased with the increase of temperature. The room temperature was chosen for experiment temperature.

As shown in Fig. S2a and Fig. S2c, the FLB intensity decreased stably with the increase of Fe (III) concentration of 0.00–20.0 mg/L and increased steadily after the increase of AA with the concentration of 0.00–10.0 mg/L adding into the BG-NCDs-Fe (III).
Therefore, the analytical performance within the above concentration range could be obtained in Fig. S2b / Fig. S2d and showed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Fe (III)/AA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The potential interferences were selected to evaluate the selectivity of this method. As shown in Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b, there was an obvious quenching on FLB by Fe (III) and effectively restored by AA with the same concentration of potential interferences adding to the system. The results indicated that the potential interferences were negligible.

The allowable interference multiple (±5%) of Fe (III) and AA detection were determined and showed in Table S2, respectively. It could be seen that the determination of Fe (III) and AA were almost unaffected even if there were a high concentration of potential interferences in the system.
The results above showed that there were a satisfactory selectivity and a good anti-interference ability of BG-NCDs for the detection of Fe (III) and AA.

There was comparison between the different methods in Table S3. It could be seen that there were lower LODs and wide linear ranges of BG-NCDs, indicating that BG-NCDs could be used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the determination of Fe (III) and AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FLG “on-off-on” determination of Cu (II) and GSH

ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions of pH, time, temperature, and ionic strength were optimized by recording the difference of fluorescence intensity (ΔFG-off, ΔFG-on) before and after adding Cu (II) and GSH under different conditions.

As shown in Fig. S4a, the effect of pH to ΔFG-off and ΔFG-on both showed a trend of increased at first and then decreased. The reason for this phenomenon probably the same as that of ΔFB-off and ΔFB-on, (1) the interaction between BG-NCDs and Cu (II) would be enhanced with the increase of pH of 3.0–7.0 (ΔFG-off↑), but the interaction would weaken with the increase of pH in the range of 7.0–11.0 because the side-reaction coefficient of hydroxyl increased in the alkaline medium(ΔFG-off↓) [35]. (2) In GSH, the thiol group (-SH) similar to the enediol group and lactone group of AA, was easily deprotonated to radical cation at suitable pH, thus binding with Cu (II) stably(ΔFG-on↑). But -SH was easily oxidized in the alkaline medium [36], resulting in the weakening of the binding ability to Cu (II) (ΔFG-on↓). Therefore, pH 7.0 was the optimum pH for the experiment.

The effect of incubation time was shown in Fig. S4b. ΔFG-off and ΔFG-on leveled to a stable value after a while. Therefore, the FLG quenching time of 5 min and recovery time of 15 min was chosen for incubation.

Through determination analysis, the ionic strength had little effect on the determination process. ΔFG-off decreased with the increase of temperature. The room temperature was chosen for experiment temperature.

The performance of BG-NCDs in the analysis of Cu (II) and GSH was investigated and shown in Fig. S5a and Fig. S5c. It could be seen that the FLG decreased continuously with the increase of Cu (II) concentration of 0.00–16.00 mg/L and gradually enhanced until it was completely recovered via the increase of the GSH concentration of 0.00–32.00 mg/L.

The FLG quenching/restore value displayed a good linear response to the concentration of Cu (II)/GSH within these ranges (Fig. S5b and Fig. S5d). The analytical performance could be obtained between FLG intensity and Cu (II)/AA concentration. The results were shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Cu (II)/GSH analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The potential interferences were selected to evaluate the selectivity of this method and show in in Fig. S6a and Fig. S6b. There was almost no response to BG-NCDs by different kinds of potential interferences with the same concentration, while Cu (II) showed obvious FLG quenching response and FLG could be effectively restored by GSH. Except for thiol-containing compounds Cys, which was a component of GSH [36], had a slight response. However, the GSH concentration outclassed Cys in biological samples, the effect of Cys on the detection could be negligible [37]. The results above could be indicated that there was a good selectivity of BG-NCDs for Cu (II) and GSH detection.

The allowable interference multiples (± 5%) of Cu (II) and GSH detection could be seen from Table S2, respectively. Which showed that there was no effect on the detection of Cu (II) and GSH even if there were a high concentration of potential interferences in the system.
These results above indicated that the BG-NCDs had satisfactory selectivity and a good anti-interference ability for the detection of Cu (II) and GSH.

There was comparison between the different methods in Table3. BG-NCDs showed lower LODs and wide liner ranges in detecting Cu (II) and GSH. It could be concluded that the “on-off-on” fluorescent probe to effectively detecting Cu (II) and GSH could be established by BG-NCDs.
In this experiment, the different real samples were determined by the “off” and “on” of the fluorescent probe which were based on the peaks of FLB and FLG, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of real samples with fluorescence probe “off”
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability of BG-NCDs in water samples, the concentrations of Cu (II) and Fe (III) in standard water samples were determined. The results of Fe (III) and Cu (II) were 3.986 mg/L and 1.991 mg/L respectively, which consistent with the standard values of Fe (III) =4.000 mg/Land Cu (II) =2.000 mg/L (95 % confidence level, n = 3). Besides, Fe (III) and Cu (II) in tap water and lake water were determined by the spiked recovery tests (Table 1 ). The recoveries of Fe (III) and Cu (II) in the two water samples were 96.5 %–103.25 % and 95.8 %–104.5 %, respectively. These results indicated that BG-NCDs had the potential capability to analyze Fe (III) and Cu (II) in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of real samples with fluorescence probe “on”
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability of BG-NCDs in food samples, determination of AA and GSH concentration in vitamin C effervescent tablets, kiwi fruit, and tomato were performed. The experiment was carried out by adding 100 μL pre-prepared fresh sample solution to the system.

Firstly, the possible interference of the samples was confirmed. The fluorescence of samples with the same concentration in detection was measured and found they showed a weak fluorescence at 350 nm. However, it could be ignored due to the high baseline of the BG-NCDs-Fe (III) and weak interference fluorescence.

The BG-NCDs-Fe (III) was used to detecting AA in vitamin C effervescent tablets and kiwifruit, the results were shown in Table 2 . The determination of concentrations were 7.23 mg/L and 4.52 mg/L with the recoveries of 102.52 %–110.70 % and 102.91 %–111.16 %, respectively. In addition, the concentration of GSH in tomatoes was detected by the BG-NCDs-Cu (II) and it was determined to be 5.08 mg/L with the recovery of 99.25 %–109.25 % (Table 2). The results above could prove the reliability of this method, implying that the method was the potential to detect AA and GSH in food samples.

The fluorescence of CDs was quenched usually through static quenching and dynamic quenching [38]. The type of quenching could be judged by Stern-Volmer quenching constant KSV firstly. The value of KSV would decrease with the increase of temperature if the quenching process of CDs was static quenching [39]. To further clarify the quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer parameters were investigated at 20 °C, 40 °C, and 60 °C. As seen from Fig. 3 a and d, the values of KSV decreased with the increase of temperature, which suggested that the quenching was caused by the static quenching mechanism.

Static quenching was usually caused by the formation of a nonfluorescent ground state complex between CDs and the quencher [38], and the quencher would change the UV–vis spectra of CDs [40]. Therefore, to judgment the formation of the nonfluorescent ground state complex in this quenching process, the UV–vis spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 3b and e, the curve of BG-NCDs + Fe (III) appeared a new absorption peak at the wavelength of 300 nm, and the absorption peak on the curve of BG-NCDs + Cu (II) was disappeared compared with UV–vis spectra of BG-NCDs. The effect of dynamic quenching could be eliminated by these phenomena and the reason may be the formation of ground state complexes between BG-NCDs and Fe (III)/Cu (II).

Then, it was further proved by the determination of the zeta potential of the quenching process in each stage for further confirmation (Fig. 3c and f). The zeta potential of BG-NCDs was measured to be -36.3 mV due to several exposed COOH on BG-NCDs surface. After adding Fe (III)/Cu (II), the zeta potential of BG-NCDs increased to 11.3 mV and -4.9 mV, respectively. Because the COOH was coordinated with Fe (III)/Cu (II) to form the ground state complex, the oxygen would obtain a positive charge led to the increase of zeta potential. This result was consistent with the UV–vis spectra. Therefore, the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between BG-NCDs and Fe (III)/Cu was the reason for the quenching of FLB and FLG could, resulting in fluorescence “off”.

The restore of fluorescence in the system was usually attributed to the competitive coordination mechanism [41]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the zeta potential of BG-NCDs decreased to 1.51mv after adding AA due to the high reducibility of AH− to Fe (III). It was consistent with the decrease of absorption peak in the UV–vis spectrum, suggesting the enediol group of AA was oxidized to an ortho-dione group of dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) with the reduction of Fe (III) to Fe(II) [34]. The chelating ability of Fe(II) to BG-NCDs was lower than Fe (III), resulting in FLB“on”.

Similarly, the negatively charged thiol group (-SH) had a stronger binding force with Cu (II) [36], resulting in the zeta potential of BG-NCDs decrease to -21.3 mV after GSH was added (Fig. 3f). The UV–vis spectra which showed in Fig. 3c also displayed the appearance of a new absorption peak. The above phenomenon indicated that Cu (II) was taken away from the surface of BG-NCDs by GSH, thereby FLG “on”.

To evaluate the applications of BG-NCDs in biomedical, the cytotoxicity to DU145 cells was conducted by MTT assay. As seen from Fig. 4 a, more than 85 % of the cells remained to survive when the concentration of BG-NCDs was added as high as 120 mg/L, which indicated that the BG-NCDs was low toxic.

Owing to these excellent properties, the BG-NCDs were applied for imaging of DU145 cells. The DU145 in Fig. 4b were incubated with BG-NCDs for 6 h, when observed in the blue channel and green channel, the cells emitted blue (Fig. 4c) and green fluorescence (Fig. 4f), respectively. However, in the control group (Fig. S7a), the shape of the cells could not be seen in either the blue channel or the green channel without BG-NCDs (Fig. S7b and Fig. S7c). It meant that BG-NCDs could enter the cells, and make the cells emit different colors of fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths. Besides, the new four groups of DU145 which were incubated with BG-NCDs as above had been prepared. Group 1 continued to incubate with Fe (III) for 2 h (Fig. S7d), Group 2 continued to incubate with Cu (II) for 2 h (Fig. S7e), Group 3 was continued to incubate with Fe (III) for 2 h and then with AA for 2 h (Fig. S7f), and the Group 4 continued to incubate with Cu (II) for 2 h and then with GSH for 2 h (Fig. S7g). When observed by the fluorescent inverted microscope, the image of Group 1 in the blue channel was shown in Fig. 4d, it could be seen that after introducing Fe (III), the FLB intensity of cells decreased significantly. However, the FLB intensity of cells in Group 3 was restored (Fig. 4e) after pretreatment with AA. Likewise, in group 3 and group 4, the presence of Cu (II) led to FLG intensity of cells decreased (Fig. 4f), and the addition of GSH could restore the FLG of cells. The results above could be concluded that BG-NCDs was feasible for multicolor imaging of DU145 cell and had great potential for prostate cancer cell imaging.

A novel nitrogen doping carbon quantum dots (BG-NCDs) was synthesized with the property of fluorescence dual-excitation to dual-emission. BG-NCDs was applied as a dual- "on-off-on" probe to the selective analysis of 4 analytes in real samples and DU145 imaging. More importantly, it opens up a new way of CDs as fluorescent probes in analytical sensing, provides a new method of cellular multicolor imaging, and maybe plays a significant role in chemical sensing and biology in the future.

Ouwen Xu has performed main jobs in this work including literature survey, experimental operation, draft-writing and data analysis. Shuyu Wan, Yuheng Zhang, Jiawei Li, were responsible for literature survey. Prof. Xiashi Zhu was responsible for result analysis and manuscript submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original, which has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375117) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions and Postgraduate research/practice innovation program of Jiangsu province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129904.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped carbon quantum dot-based “off-on” fluorescent sensor for silver ion and cysteine
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liao, X. Zhao, F. Zhu, M. Chen, Z. Wu, X. Song, H. Yang, X. Chen, Novel S, N-doped carbon quantum dot-based “off-on” fluorescent sensor for silver ion and cysteine, Talanta. 180 (2018) 300–308. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2017.12.040.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr Ouwen Xu is a master student at the University of Yangzhou University, China. 1172535292@qq.com
Ms Shuyu Wan is a master student at the University of Yangzhou University, China. 2607338592@qq.com
Mr Yuheng Zhang is a PhD student at the University of Yangzhou University, China. 365818545@qq.com
Mr Jiawei Li is a master student at the University of Yangzhou University, China. 291554526@qq.com

Prof Xiashi Zhu is a Professor of University of Yangzhou University, China. Her teaching interests are in the fields of analytical chemistry. Currently her research focuses on spectral analysis. xszhu@yzu.edu.cn
